statusLine: Americas crude markets held steady at $105.42 WTI while inventory draws and Middle East shipping disruptions support elevated pricing despite record US production.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets remained flat in the week ending May 17, with WTI unchanged at $105.42/barrel, maintaining pressure on American consumers facing gasoline prices near $4.63/gallon. The stability comes despite significant inventory draws, with commercial crude stocks falling 8.6 million barrels and gasoline inventories dropping 4.1 million barrels, suggesting robust domestic demand is absorbing supply even as US production reached a new record high of 13.71 million barrels per day.

The $3.84 discount of WTI to Brent reflects ongoing global supply concerns, particularly around critical shipping chokepoints in the Middle East. Multiple CRITICAL maritime advisories remain active for Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait, as well as Iranian threats in the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz. These disruptions are forcing longer tanker routes and supporting the Brent premium, indirectly benefiting Americas producers who can deliver oil without navigating these risk zones.

US shale operators continue to demonstrate resilience, with production climbing even as prices have stabilized above $100/barrel. The combination of record output and inventory draws indicates the domestic market is absorbing supply efficiently, though retail fuel prices remain elevated with gasoline up 4.7 cents to $4.628/gallon while diesel held steady. The 81.1 million barrel Strategic Petroleum Reserve provides limited buffer capacity compared to historical levels.

Across the broader Americas, the elevated WTI pricing environment benefits Canadian oil sands producers despite transportation discounts, while supporting continued investment in Guyana's rapidly expanding Stabroek field and Brazil's pre-salt developments. Venezuelan production remains constrained by sanctions, removing a key regional supplier from normal market dynamics and supporting current price levels.
keyPoints: WTI crude held steady at $105.42/barrel in week ending May 17, unchanged from prior week
US crude production hit record 13.71 million bpd while commercial inventories fell 8.6 million barrels
Gasoline prices rose to $4.628/gallon amid 4.1 million barrel inventory draw, pressuring consumers
Critical shipping disruptions in Red Sea and Persian Gulf support $3.84 WTI-Brent discount
Strategic Petroleum Reserve at 81.1 million barrels provides limited emergency buffer capacity
Record US shale output demonstrates producer resilience above $100/barrel price level
